ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general almond entry; roasted and salted preparation variant is not separately coded. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers almonds as a commodity; roasted and salted preparation facet not separately enumerated. Closest defensible code used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT substance code for almond used; roasted/salted preparation not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of roasted salted almonds is approximately 30g (about 23 whole almonds), providing roughly 182 kcal, 6.4g protein, 16.6g fat, and 5.3g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted almond, salted — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F09030001 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken from this source without modification. USDA FoodData Central (NDB No. 12063, Nuts, almonds, dry roasted, with salt added) consulted as secondary cross-reference; values are consistent.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of almonds is reported as approximately 0–15 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008). Roasted salted almonds are used here as the closest analogous form; roasting and salting do not materially alter the GI of almonds. GL per serving calculated as: GI (15) × carbohydrate per serving (5.3g) / 100 = 0.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 339mg/100g (borderline high) offset by potassium 705mg/100g and dietary fibre 10.5g/100g
hypertension.guidance: Limit portion to 30g per serving (~100mg sodium) and consider unsalted almonds if hypertension is poorly controlled; the high potassium-to-sodium ratio and fibre content support DASH diet principles and favour blood pressure management at controlled portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (~15), high fibre (10.5g/100g), high protein (21.2g/100g), and healthy unsaturated fats improve insulin sensitivity and slow gastric emptying
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted salted almonds are suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management at a 30g serving; their very low GI, high fibre, and protein content blunt postprandial glucose response, but caloric density (607 kcal/100g) requires portion control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High MUFA (34.7g/100g) and PUFA (13.7g/100g) with low saturated fat (4.2g/100g); rich in vitamin E (25.6mg/100g), magnesium, and fibre
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Almonds are a cardioprotective food supported by clinical evidence; a 30g daily serving reduces LDL cholesterol and improves lipid profiles, though the added salt (339mg Na/100g) warrants portion control or substitution with unsalted almonds for patients with concurrent hypertension.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High MUFA/PUFA ratio qualifies for whole-nut clinical carve-out; vitamin E (25.6mg/100g) provides anti-inflammatory antioxidant activity; fibre 10.5g/100g supports immune regulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted salted almonds are suitable for rheumatoid arthritis at a 30g serving due to their dominant unsaturated fat profile and vitamin E content; choose unsalted almonds to avoid sodium-related tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted salted almonds are whole almonds (Prunus dulcis) that have been dry-roasted to develop a toasted, crunchy texture and seasoned with salt. They are one of the most nutritionally dense snack foods available, providing 607 kcal per 100g with an exceptional macronutrient profile: 21.2g protein, 55.2g fat (predominantly heart-healthy monounsaturated fat at ~34.7g/100g and polyunsaturated fat at ~13.7g/100g), 17.7g carbohydrate, and 10.5g dietary fibre. The saturated fat content is low at approximately 4.2g/100g. Sodium is 339mg/100g due to added salt, which is in the borderline zone for hypertension management. Almonds are exceptionally rich in vitamin E (25.6mg/100g), magnesium (270mg/100g), potassium (705mg/100g), calcium (291mg/100g), and phosphorus (484mg/100g). Their glycemic index is very low (~15), making them highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management. The high potassium-to-sodium ratio and fibre content support DASH diet principles for hypertension. Clinically, a 30g daily serving of almonds has been shown to reduce LDL cholesterol, improve insulin sensitivity, and support weight management through satiety. They are vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and suitable for halal and kosher diets. Roasted salted almonds are widely consumed as a standalone snack, in trail mixes, on salads, and as a topping for desserts and yogurt bowls.
common_user_queries: Are roasted salted almonds good for diabetics?
How many almonds should I eat per day?
Are salted almonds bad for blood pressure?
Calories in roasted salted almonds per 100g
Can I eat almonds if I have hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F09030001, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central (dry roasted almonds with salt) confirms consistency. Fatty acid breakdown, vitamins, and minerals supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the closest approved secondary source. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed publication.
notes: Saturated fat (4.18g), MUFA (34.66g), PUFA (13.66g), trans fat (0.0g), potassium (705mg), cholesterol (0.0mg), vitamin C (0.0mg), vitamin A (0.0 mcg RAE), B-vitamins, vitamin E (25.63mg), phosphorus (484mg), magnesium (270mg), zinc (3.08mg), selenium (4.1mcg), and water (2.53g) supplemented from USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 12063 (Nuts, almonds, dry roasted, with salt added) as the closest approved secondary source, consistent with HPB primary values. Cholesterol is confirmed zero as almonds are a plant food containing no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin B12 and vitamin D are confirmed zero as almonds contain negligible amounts of these nutrients. Trans fat is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; roasting and salting do not materially alter almond GI. The dietary tag 'wholegrain' in the input is not applicable to almonds (nuts are not grains) and has been omitted. No multilingual local-language names are required as this is a globally generic food item with no specific Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese cuisine classification.
